context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh this spider diagram thing lol, idk what it’s even asking me to put for the 5 pathways.
exchanges.tutor:
Start with the assignment’s core task: it wants you to organize what your case study city is doing about climate change under each of the 5 pathways. 

Before we go further: what is your city, and do you have the names of the 5 pathways from your course page in front of you? If yes, send them, and we’ll sort your city’s actions into the right buckets one by one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the reply brief, stay focused on the spider diagram assignment, and use guided discovery rather than listing the content for the student. Since the student is confused about the task itself, I should first orient them to the assignment structure and ask a targeted question that moves them toward identifying what the diagram wants: evidence from their chosen city organized under the 5 pathways. I will not fill in the diagram for them or write any submission language.

exchanges.student: i have the city, but tbh the pathway names are blurring together rn and idk what counts as an “action” vs just like city background.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—focus on the distinction first.

Try this test: is the item something the city or a major actor is actually doing, funding, regulating, planning, or building? If yes, it’s probably an action. If it just describes the city’s context, risk, size, economy, or politics, that’s background.

Give me 2–3 things you found about your city, and I’ll ask you which side each belongs on.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for conceptual clarification, not a completed solution. I should define the task boundary indirectly by contrasting categories through questions and a simple test, while keeping the response bite-sized. This supports guided discovery and helps them separate descriptive background from concrete climate actions without filling in their diagram.

exchanges.student: that’s where i’m stuck tho bc everything kinda sounds like both lol. like heat waves, a climate plan, and a new bus line all seem related so idk.
exchanges.tutor:
They’re related, but not the same kind of thing.

Ask: which one is the problem/pressure, and which ones are responses?
- Heat waves: is that something the city is doing, or something happening to the city?
- Climate plan: document/background, or a deliberate response tool?
- New bus line: condition, or an intervention?

Try sorting those three into “context/problem” vs “action/response.”
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is conflating context and action. I should use guided discovery on the specific examples they provided rather than naming answers outright. I will contrast 'condition/problem' versus 'response' with short Socratic prompts that move toward sorting items for the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: ok so heat waves = problem, bus line = action, but the climate plan still messes me up bc it’s a document not like an actual project fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Better. Don’t overcomplicate it.

Ask yourself: does the climate plan commit the city to targets, policies, budgets, timelines, or agencies? If so, is it just background—or is it part of how the city acts and organizes action?

What does your city’s plan actually contain: goals only, or concrete measures too?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made progress and is stuck on one nuanced item. I should acknowledge the correct distinctions and push them to think about whether a plan counts as action in governance terms. I will avoid directly answering and instead ask criteria-based questions that help them decide how to use it in the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: idk yet, i haven’t read the whole thing and that’s kinda why i’m overwhelmed lol. can u just explain the 5 pathways in a simple way bc they all sound the same rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—treat the 5 pathways as 5 different lenses for the same city.

Ask of any item:
1. Does this change the city’s physical/built systems?
2. Does this change nature/ecosystems or land use?
3. Does this change institutions, policy, or governance?
4. Does this change everyday social practices, behavior, or equity?
5. Does this change the economy, finance, or jobs/investment?

They overlap, but usually one lens fits best. Want to test one item from your city against those 5 questions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and needs orientation. I should reduce cognitive load by giving a high-level, non-solution framing of the five pathways without filling their diagram or writing their assignment. To stay Socratic, I’ll present them as distinguishing questions the student can use, then ask the student to classify one city item. This keeps the discussion on-task and moves toward the solution.

exchanges.student: still kinda no bc now i feel like literally every item could fit 3 of those, esp transit stuff :/
exchanges.tutor:
That’s normal. The point isn’t perfect purity; it’s strongest fit.

Use this rule: put the item where its main mechanism sits. For transit, ask: is the key thing infrastructure, policy/governance, social access, or financing?

Pick one transit item from your city. What exactly is being changed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck in overlap anxiety. I should narrow the task by emphasizing that the goal is categorization by strongest fit, not perfect exclusivity, and use a concrete decision rule. I must stay brief, avoid solving their assignment, and ask one question that advances classification.

exchanges.student: see that’s what i mean, “main mechanism” is kinda abstract to me ngl. can u give me like a super basic example with one city thing and how u decide, bc my brain’s fried.
exchanges.tutor:
Sure. Generic example: a city builds a protected bike-lane network.

Ask: what is the city primarily doing? It’s changing streets/infrastructure. So the strongest fit is the built/physical systems pathway.

Could it also affect equity, behavior, or emissions? Yes. But those are effects. The pathway is based on the main lever.

Now use that same test on one item from your city: what is the city actually changing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and needs a simpler heuristic. Per the rules, I can be less Socratic and use a relevant example, but I still must not solve their assignment or provide a complete answer. I’ll give one generic example and model the reasoning process, then immediately ask them to apply the same logic to their own city item.

exchanges.student: ok but then if it’s a climate action plan, the “thing” being changed is like… governance maybe? i’m not totally sure.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re thinking in the right direction.

Test it: does the plan organize decision-making—targets, departments, coordination, accountability, policy direction? If yes, what pathway does that sound closest to?

Answer in one phrase, not a full explanation.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reaching the likely classification and needs confirmation through guided discovery, not a direct answer. I should validate the reasoning pathway and ask a short follow-up that tests whether the plan functions as governance. This keeps the student doing the cognitive work.

exchanges.student: maybe governance/institutions? but idk if that’s too obvious or too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor:
Not too simplistic—if you can justify it, it’s usable.

Now tighten it: what feature of the plan makes you place it there—targets, agencies, implementation steps, reporting, coordination?

Pick one concrete feature. That’s what will make your diagram defensible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached a plausible categorization but lacks confidence. I should affirm the reasoning process without declaring a final answer, reinforce that simple and defensible is acceptable, and prompt them to justify their choice with evidence from the plan. This maintains the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and keeps the response brief.

exchanges.student: umm probably targets? but i still feel like i’m guessing half of this tbh. how detailed does the spider diagram even need to be?
exchanges.tutor:
It does not need to be exhaustive. It needs to show that you can map your city’s climate action across the 5 pathways with specific, relevant items.

A solid entry is usually: one concrete action or plan element + a short note on why it fits that pathway.

So don’t chase every detail. Aim for clear, defensible examples. Want to draft just one pathway entry now in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about scope/detail, which is appropriate. I should reduce anxiety by reframing the assignment as a concise mapping exercise, not a polished essay, and give minimal criteria for sufficient detail without composing content for them. I will keep it bite-sized and end with a question that directs them to produce one pathway entry.
